name: Egg McMuffin (McDonald's)
popularityContext: McDonald's flagship breakfast sandwich, introduced in 1971 and still one of the most-ordered fast-food breakfast items in America; a household name for grab-and-go morning meals.
prepNotes: Toast a split English muffin with butter. Cook a cracked egg in a round mold until set, warm the Canadian bacon, then layer bacon, cheese, and egg between the muffin halves. Ready in under 10 minutes; this is also the basis of countless homemade copycat versions.

nutrition.method: Published nutrition panel, McDonald's USA (verified against USDA FoodData Central for component foods)

regionAvailability.notes: Sold at McDonald's restaurants nationwide; English muffins, Canadian bacon, and American cheese for a homemade version are available at every major US supermarket.

name: Starbucks Bacon, Gouda & Egg Sandwich
popularityContext: One of Starbucks' best-selling hot breakfast sandwiches, ordered alongside coffee by millions of US commuters as a quick on-the-way-to-work breakfast.
prepNotes: This is a chain menu item assembled and warmed in-store; for a homemade version, bake an egg-Parmesan frittata patty, layer it on a toasted roll with smoked bacon and sliced Gouda, and warm under a broiler until the cheese melts.

nutrition.method: Published nutrition panel, Starbucks USA menu

regionAvailability.notes: Sold at Starbucks locations nationwide, including in-store cafes inside Target stores.

name: Dunkin' Bacon, Egg & Cheese Wake-Up Wrap
popularityContext: Dunkin's compact Wake-Up Wrap is a staple grab-and-go item across its US drive-thru locations, popular with commuters who want something smaller and cheaper than a full sandwich.
prepNotes: Chain item: scrambled egg, crisped bacon, and a small portion of American cheese are rolled into a soft 6-inch flour tortilla and griddled until warm. Easy to replicate at home in about 5 minutes using a nonstick skillet.

nutrition.method: Published nutrition panel, Dunkin' USA menu

regionAvailability.notes: Sold at Dunkin' locations nationwide; tortillas, eggs, bacon, and cheese for a homemade version are basic items at any US grocery store.

name: Diner-Style Two Eggs, Bacon, Hash Browns and Toast
popularityContext: The quintessential American diner breakfast plate, found on nearly every diner and greasy-spoon menu nationwide since the mid-20th century; the benchmark 'classic American breakfast.'
prepNotes: Fry eggs in butter to preference (over-easy is classic diner style). Cook bacon until crisp and pan-fry shredded hash browns until golden. Toast bread and butter while everything is hot. Plate together; this is the diner standard nationwide, with only minor regional variation in starch (grits in the South, home fries in the Northeast).

nutrition.method: Calculated from ingredient composition via USDA FoodData Central

regionAvailability.notes: All ingredients are everyday staples sold at every US grocery chain; this exact plate is also on nearly every diner menu in the country (Denny's, Waffle House, IHOP, local diners).

name: Biscuits and Sausage Gravy
popularityContext: A Southern American breakfast classic dating to the 19th century, when it began as an inexpensive, filling meal for farming and mill-working families; still a staple on Southern diner and home breakfast tables today.
prepNotes: Brown the sausage in a skillet, then sprinkle in flour and stir to form a roux with the drippings. Gradually whisk in milk and simmer until thickened into a creamy gravy, seasoning generously with black pepper. Split a warm biscuit and ladle the gravy over the top.

nutrition.method: Calculated from ingredient composition via USDA FoodData Central

regionAvailability.notes: Biscuit mix, breakfast sausage, and milk are sold at every US grocery store; especially popular and widely available across the South and Midwest, including at Cracker Barrel and Bojangles chains.

name: Chorizo, Egg and Potato Breakfast Burrito
popularityContext: A Tex-Mex breakfast staple across the Southwest and increasingly nationwide, sold at taquerias, gas stations, and chains alike; rooted in Mexican-American border cuisine.
prepNotes: Brown chorizo in a skillet, breaking it up, then scramble in the eggs until just set. Roast or pan-fry diced potatoes separately until crisp. Warm the tortilla, layer chorizo-egg, potatoes, cheese, and salsa down the center, fold, and roll tightly burrito-style.

name: New York Bagel with Lox and Cream Cheese
popularityContext: An iconic New York City Jewish-deli breakfast dating back to early-20th-century immigrant communities; now sold at bagel shops and delis across the country as a weekend brunch classic.
prepNotes: Slice the bagel in half and toast lightly if desired. Spread both halves generously with cream cheese, layer smoked salmon over one half, and top with thin red onion and capers before closing or serving open-faced.

nutrition.method: Calculated from ingredient composition via USDA FoodData Central

name: High-Protein Overnight Oats with Greek Yogurt and Berries
popularityContext: A defining dish of the meal-prep and high-protein breakfast trend that grew rapidly on US social media and fitness blogs over the past decade, valued for being make-ahead and portable.
prepNotes: Stir oats, Greek yogurt, milk, chia seeds, and honey together in a jar or container. Cover and refrigerate overnight (at least 6 hours). Top with mixed berries before eating; keeps well in the fridge for up to 4-5 days, making it easy to batch for the week.

name: Starbucks Bacon & Gruyère Sous Vide Egg Bites
popularityContext: Sous vide egg bites became a viral Starbucks menu item and a widely copied meal-prep format after launching in 2017, prized for being portable, high-protein, and low-carb.
prepNotes: Blend eggs and cottage cheese until smooth, then stir in shredded Gruyère and chopped cooked bacon. Pour into silicone muffin molds and bake in a water bath (sous vide style) at 300°F for about 30 minutes until set. Two bites per serving, just like the Starbucks packaging.

name: Turkey Sausage Links with Scrambled Eggs and Fruit
popularityContext: A common lighter alternative to a traditional pork-sausage breakfast plate, widely chosen across the US by people looking for a familiar diner-style breakfast with a leaner protein.
prepNotes: Pan-fry turkey sausage links until browned and cooked through, about 6-8 minutes. Scramble eggs in butter in a separate pan until just set. Plate with a side of fresh mixed fruit in place of toast or potatoes for a lighter version of the classic diner plate.

nutrition.method: Calculated from ingredient composition via USDA FoodData Central

regionAvailability.notes: Turkey breakfast sausage links, eggs, and fresh fruit are sold nationwide at every major US grocery chain year-round.
